Definition: A service for centrally managing multiple AWS accounts — Organizational Units (OUs) for hierarchical grouping, Service Control Policies (SCPs) for permission guardrails, consolidated billing with volume discounts, and integration with Control Tower, RAM, and CloudFormation StackSets.

Organization-level permission boundaries — restrict (not grant) what IAM policies can allow in member accounts. Inherited hierarchically through OUs, don't affect the management account, and support deny lists (deny specific actions) or allow lists (allow only specific actions) strategies.

An automated multi-account governance service — providing landing zones (pre-configured multi-account environment), guardrails (preventive SCPs + detective Config rules), Account Factory (standardized account provisioning), and a compliance dashboard across the organization.

Separating workloads across accounts for security, billing, and blast radius isolation — common patterns: per-environment (dev/staging/prod), per-team, per-application, shared services account, security/audit account, log archive account, and network hub account.

Sharing AWS resources across accounts without duplication — sharing VPC subnets, Transit Gateways, Route 53 Resolver rules, License Manager configurations, and more. Integrates with Organizations for automatic sharing to all accounts in an OU.

IAM roles with trust policies for cross-account assume role, resource-based policies (S3, SQS, SNS, KMS, Lambda), RAM for resource sharing, Organizations delegated administrator, and AWS SSO (IAM Identity Center) for centralized human access across accounts.

Centralized workforce identity management — single sign-on to multiple AWS accounts and business applications, integrating with Active Directory or external IdPs (Okta, Azure AD), permission sets mapped to accounts, and SAML 2.0/OIDC federation.

Route 53 Resolver for hybrid DNS — Inbound endpoints (on-premises resolves AWS domains), Outbound endpoints (AWS resolves on-premises domains), Resolver rules (forwarding rules for specific domains), and shared rules across accounts via RAM.

A regional network hub — connecting hundreds of VPCs, VPNs, Direct Connect gateways, and peered Transit Gateways. Features: route tables for network segmentation, multicast support, inter-region peering, and centralized network management replacing complex VPC peering meshes.

Network segmentation via multiple route tables (shared services, isolated, blackhole routes), Transit Gateway Connect for SD-WAN integration, multicast domains, appliance mode for stateful inspection, and inter-region peering for global network architectures.

Dedicated private connectivity — 1/10/100 Gbps dedicated or 50 Mbps-10 Gbps hosted connections. Virtual interfaces: Public VIF (AWS public services), Private VIF (single VPC), Transit VIF (Transit Gateway). LAG for link aggregation, and MACsec for Layer 2 encryption.

High availability patterns — Maximum Resiliency (separate connections at separate locations), High Resiliency (two connections at two locations), and Development/Test (single connection). Use Site-to-Site VPN as encrypted backup over internet for failover.

A globally available resource connecting Direct Connect to multiple VPCs across regions — supporting up to 10 VGWs or Transit Gateways. Eliminates the need for separate Direct Connect connections per region.

Term: VPC Peering vs Transit Gateway vs PrivateLink
Definition: VPC Peering: point-to-point, non-transitive, no bandwidth limit, free same-AZ. Transit Gateway: hub-and-spoke, transitive, centralized routing, per-attachment cost. PrivateLink: service-to-consumer, unidirectional, NLB-backed, keeps traffic private — choose based on connectivity pattern.

A managed stateful/stateless firewall for VPCs — IPS/IDS, domain filtering, protocol detection, and Suricata-compatible rules. Deployed in dedicated firewall subnets with centralized management via Firewall Manager across accounts in Organizations.

Aggregating logs from all accounts — CloudTrail organization trail to central S3 bucket, CloudWatch Logs cross-account subscriptions via Kinesis, VPC Flow Logs to central S3, Config aggregator, and Security Hub delegated administrator for centralized findings.

Enforcing consistent tagging across Organizations — tag policies define allowed tag keys/values, AWS Config rules detect non-compliant tags, SCPs can require tags on resource creation, and cost allocation tags enable chargeback/showback reporting.

Deploying infrastructure across multiple accounts and regions — service-managed (Organizations integration, auto-deploy to new accounts) or self-managed permissions. Supports concurrent deployment, failure tolerance, and automatic drift detection.

Centralized security posture — aggregating findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Firewall Manager, Config, and third-party tools. Automated compliance checks (CIS, PCI DSS, AWS FSBP), cross-account aggregation via Organizations, and custom actions for remediation.

Intelligent threat detection — analyzing CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, EKS audit logs, S3 data events, and RDS login activity. ML-based detection of compromised instances, unauthorized access, cryptocurrency mining, and data exfiltration across all accounts.

Collecting Config data across multiple accounts and regions — providing a centralized compliance view. Organization aggregator (automatic for all accounts) or individual account authorization. Used with conformance packs for organization-wide compliance.

Exposing services privately across VPCs and accounts — service provider creates NLB + VPC endpoint service, consumers create interface VPC endpoints. Traffic stays on AWS network, no VPC peering/Transit Gateway needed, supports cross-account and AWS Marketplace services.

Event-driven, pay-per-use architectures — API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB (REST APIs), S3 + Lambda (file processing), EventBridge + Step Functions (orchestration), SQS + Lambda (async processing), and AppSync + DynamoDB (GraphQL APIs).

Serverless compute — up to 15 min execution, 10 GB memory, 10 GB /tmp, provisioned concurrency (eliminate cold starts), reserved concurrency (throttle limit), Lambda@Edge/CloudFront Functions, container image support, SnapStart (Java), and Lambda Layers for shared code.

REST APIs (full features, request/response transformation), HTTP APIs (lower cost, simpler, faster), WebSocket APIs (real-time). Features: custom domains, mutual TLS, usage plans/API keys, caching, canary deployments, and WAF integration.

Serverless workflow orchestration — Standard (up to 1 year, exactly-once, audit history) and Express (up to 5 min, at-least-once, high volume). States: Task, Choice, Parallel, Map (dynamic parallelism), Wait, Pass, Succeed, Fail. Error handling with Retry and Catch.

Serverless event bus — event patterns for filtering, input transformation, archive/replay for debugging, schema registry for discovery, cross-account/cross-region event delivery, Pipes for point-to-point integration, and Scheduler for one-time/recurring invocations.

Managed container orchestration — EC2 launch type (you manage instances, more control) vs Fargate (serverless, per-task billing). Task definitions, services with desired count, ALB/NLB integration, service discovery (Cloud Map), auto-scaling, and capacity providers.

Managed Kubernetes — control plane across 3 AZs, EC2/Fargate/Outposts node types, managed node groups, Kubernetes-native tooling (kubectl, Helm), IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), EKS Anywhere for hybrid, and EKS Blueprints for standardized clusters.

ECS: AWS-native, simpler, tighter AWS integration, lower operational overhead. EKS: Kubernetes-compatible, portable across clouds, rich ecosystem, complex but flexible. Choose ECS for AWS-centric workloads; EKS when Kubernetes compatibility or multi-cloud portability is required.

MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible — 6 copies across 3 AZs, up to 128 TB auto-scaling storage, 15 read replicas, Global Database (sub-second cross-region replication), Serverless v2 (instant scaling), Multi-Master (multi-writer), Backtrack (rewind without restore), and cloning.

Serverless NoSQL — partition key design for even distribution, GSI/LSI for query flexibility, DynamoDB Streams (CDC for Lambda triggers), TTL for automatic expiration, DAX (microsecond caching), Global Tables (multi-region active-active), and on-demand vs provisioned capacity.

Relational (RDS/Aurora): ACID, complex queries, joins. Key-value (DynamoDB): single-digit ms, massive scale. Document (DocumentDB): MongoDB-compatible. Graph (Neptune): relationships. Time-series (Timestream): IoT/metrics. Ledger (QLDB): immutable, verifiable. In-memory (ElastiCache): caching.

Redis: persistence (RDB/AOF), replication, Multi-AZ failover, pub/sub, sorted sets, Lua scripting, cluster mode (sharding). Memcached: multi-threaded, simple, no persistence. Use Redis for complex data structures and HA; Memcached for simple caching with multi-threaded performance.

Data Streams (custom consumers, 24h-365d retention, enhanced fan-out), Firehose (managed delivery to S3/Redshift/OpenSearch/Splunk with transformation), Data Analytics (SQL/Flink real-time processing), and Video Streams (video ingestion). Shard-based scaling for Data Streams.

Term: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka)
Definition: A managed Apache Kafka service — for high-throughput event streaming, log aggregation, and real-time analytics. MSK Serverless (auto-scaling, no capacity management) vs MSK Provisioned (control broker count/type). Choose MSK over Kinesis when Kafka compatibility is required.

S3 (storage) + Lake Formation (governance/security) + Glue (ETL/catalog) + Athena (ad-hoc SQL) + Redshift Spectrum (warehouse queries on S3) + EMR (big data processing) + QuickSight (visualization). Kinesis/MSK for real-time ingestion, Macie for data security.

Columnar data warehouse — RA3 nodes (managed storage, scale compute independently), Serverless (auto-scaling), Spectrum (query S3 directly), Concurrency Scaling (burst read capacity), materialized views, federated queries (RDS/Aurora), and cross-region data sharing.

Serverless ETL — Crawlers (schema discovery), Jobs (Spark/Python Shell), DataBrew (visual prep), Elastic Views (materialized views across data stores), Schema Registry (Avro/JSON schema versioning), and Glue Studio (visual ETL authoring with job monitoring).

S3 Object Lambda (transform data on retrieval), S3 Batch Operations (bulk operations on billions of objects), S3 Multi-Region Access Points (global endpoint with automatic routing), S3 Object Lock (WORM), S3 Inventory, S3 Analytics, and S3 Event Notifications.

Origin groups (failover between origins), field-level encryption, signed URLs/cookies (private content), real-time logs to Kinesis, Origin Access Control (OAC) for S3, custom headers for origin verification, and cache policies vs origin request policies.

Network-level optimization via AWS global network — static Anycast IPs, automatic regional failover, endpoint weights for traffic distribution, client affinity, and health checks. Use for non-HTTP (TCP/UDP), static IPs, or deterministic routing vs CloudFront for content caching.

Backup & Restore (RPO/RTO hours, lowest cost), Pilot Light (core running, minutes RTO), Warm Standby (scaled-down full copy, minutes RTO), Multi-Site Active-Active (near-zero RPO/RTO, highest cost). Key services: Route 53 failover, S3 CRR, Aurora Global DB, DynamoDB Global Tables.

Full workload in multiple regions — Route 53 latency/weighted routing, DynamoDB Global Tables (multi-active writes), Aurora Global Database (write forwarding), CloudFront with multi-region origins, S3 Multi-Region Access Points, and Global Accelerator for failover.

SQS (queue-based, pull), SNS (pub/sub, push), EventBridge (event routing, rules), Kinesis (streaming, replay), Step Functions (orchestration), and SQS + SNS fan-out. Choose based on: synchronous vs async, ordering needs, replay requirements, and consumer patterns.

CloudFront (edge caching), ElastiCache (application-level), DAX (DynamoDB-specific), API Gateway caching (API responses). Patterns: cache-aside/lazy loading (read), write-through (write), TTL-based expiration. Consider cache invalidation, consistency, and hit ratio.

Standard (unlimited throughput, at-least-once) vs FIFO (300/3,000 TPS, exactly-once, ordering). Features: visibility timeout, DLQ (failed message isolation), delay queues, long polling (reduce empty responses), message groups (FIFO parallelism), and temporary queues.

A managed GraphQL API service — real-time subscriptions via WebSocket, offline data sync, resolver mapping templates, pipeline resolvers, caching, multiple data sources (DynamoDB, Lambda, RDS, OpenSearch, HTTP), and fine-grained authorization (Cognito, IAM, API key, OIDC).

AWS IoT Core (MQTT/HTTP device connectivity), IoT Rules Engine (routing to services), IoT Greengrass (edge compute/ML), IoT Analytics (time-series analysis), IoT Events (event detection), IoT SiteWise (industrial data), and IoT TwinMaker (digital twins).

SageMaker (full ML lifecycle), Comprehend (NLP), Rekognition (computer vision), Textract (document extraction), Translate (translation), Polly (text-to-speech), Transcribe (speech-to-text), Forecast (time series), Personalize (recommendations), and Kendra (intelligent search).

Shared NFS file system — Standard and One Zone storage classes, lifecycle policies (IA tiering), throughput modes (Bursting, Provisioned, Elastic), performance modes (General Purpose, Max I/O), encryption at rest/transit, and access points for application-specific entry points.

FSx for Windows (SMB, AD, NTFS, DFS), FSx for Lustre (HPC, ML, S3 integration, sub-ms latency), FSx for NetApp ONTAP (multi-protocol NFS/SMB/iSCSI, snapshots, clones), FSx for OpenZFS (Linux, snapshots, compression). Choose based on protocol, OS, and workload requirements.

Term: AWS Secrets Manager vs Parameter Store
Definition: Secrets Manager: automatic rotation, cross-account sharing, RDS/Aurora native rotation, higher cost. Parameter Store: free standard tier, hierarchical storage, no native rotation (use Lambda), simpler. Use Secrets Manager for database credentials; Parameter Store for configuration data.

KMS (managed keys, envelope encryption, key policies), CloudHSM (dedicated HSMs, FIPS 140-2 L3), ACM (SSL/TLS certificates), S3 encryption (SSE-S3/SSE-KMS/SSE-C/client-side), EBS encryption (KMS-backed), and RDS/Aurora encryption (KMS, TDE for Oracle/SQL Server).

Retire (decommission), Retain (keep as-is), Rehost (lift-and-shift), Relocate (hypervisor-level move), Replatform (lift-tinker-and-shift), Refactor/Re-architect (cloud-native redesign), and Repurchase (move to SaaS). Choose based on business value, complexity, and timeline.

A central tracking service for application migrations — monitoring progress across AWS migration tools (MGN, DMS, SMS), providing discovery data from Application Discovery Service, and offering migration strategy recommendations.

The primary lift-and-shift migration service — continuous block-level replication from source servers to AWS, non-disruptive testing, and cutover with minimal downtime. Supports physical, virtual, and cloud servers to EC2. Replaces CloudEndure Migration.

Migrating databases with minimal downtime — supporting homogeneous (Oracle→Oracle) and heterogeneous (Oracle→Aurora) migrations. Features: continuous replication (CDC), Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) for schema translation, and multi-AZ for HA.

Converting database schemas between engines — transforming stored procedures, functions, views, and triggers from source (Oracle, SQL Server) to target (Aurora, PostgreSQL, MySQL). Identifies conversion issues and provides assessment reports.

An online data transfer service — moving data between on-premises storage (NFS, SMB, HDFS, self-managed object storage) and AWS (S3, EFS, FSx) at speeds up to 10 Gbps. Automatic encryption, integrity validation, scheduling, and bandwidth throttling.

Managed file transfer to/from S3 and EFS — supporting SFTP, FTPS, FTP, and AS2 protocols. Integrates with existing authentication systems (AD, LDAP, custom Lambda), provides static endpoints, and enables migration of file-based workflows without changing client applications.

Physical data transfer devices — Snowcone (8-14 TB, portable, edge compute), Snowball Edge Storage Optimized (80 TB, edge compute), Snowball Edge Compute Optimized (42 TB, powerful compute), Snowmobile (100 PB, exabyte-scale). Use when network transfer would take weeks/months.

Internet (small data, quick), Direct Connect (consistent, large ongoing), DataSync (scheduled, automated), Transfer Family (protocol-based), Snow Family (massive offline), S3 Transfer Acceleration (global uploads), and Kinesis/MSK (real-time streaming). Choose based on volume, frequency, and latency.

Right-sizing with Compute Optimizer, Graviton instances (40% better price-performance), Spot for fault-tolerant workloads, Auto Scaling for demand matching, placement groups for low latency, and ENA/EFA for enhanced networking.

EBS: gp3 over gp2, io2 for high IOPS, RAID 0 for throughput. S3: multipart upload, byte-range fetches, Transfer Acceleration. EFS: Max I/O mode for high parallelism. FSx for Lustre: HPC workloads with S3 integration.

Read replicas for read scaling, ElastiCache/DAX for caching, Aurora Serverless for variable workloads, DynamoDB auto-scaling with DAX, Redshift Concurrency Scaling for burst queries, and connection pooling (RDS Proxy) for Lambda/serverless.

A fully managed database proxy — connection pooling for RDS/Aurora, reducing database connection overhead from Lambda functions and applications. Supports IAM authentication, Secrets Manager integration, and automatic failover for improved availability.

Reserved Instances/Savings Plans (baseline), Spot (fault-tolerant), right-sizing (Compute Optimizer), S3 lifecycle policies, serverless for variable workloads, Aurora Serverless for intermittent DB, and consolidated billing volume discounts across Organizations.

ML-powered recommendations for EC2 instances, EBS volumes, Lambda functions, and ECS/Fargate — analyzing CloudWatch metrics to identify over/under-provisioned resources with projected savings and performance impact ratings.

Monolith to microservices (Strangler Fig pattern), EC2 to containers (ECS/EKS), containers to serverless (Lambda), self-managed DB to managed (RDS/Aurora), batch to event-driven (EventBridge/Lambda), and on-premises to cloud-native services.

Incrementally migrating a monolith to microservices — routing specific functionality to new services while the monolith handles the rest. Use API Gateway or ALB path-based routing to gradually shift traffic until the monolith is fully replaced.

Six pillars — Operational Excellence (automate, learn from failures), Security (least privilege, defense in depth), Reliability (recover, scale, manage change), Performance Efficiency (right resources, experiment), Cost Optimization (eliminate waste), Sustainability (minimize impact).

Automated best-practice checks across 5 categories — Cost Optimization, Performance, Security, Fault Tolerance, Service Limits. Full checks require Business/Enterprise Support. Integrates with EventBridge for automated remediation workflows.

CloudFormation/CDK for IaC, Systems Manager for fleet management, Config for compliance, CloudWatch for observability, X-Ray for tracing, EventBridge for event-driven automation, and CI/CD pipelines (CodePipeline/CodeDeploy) for safe deployments.

CodeCommit/GitHub (source) → CodeBuild (build/test) → CodeDeploy (deploy to EC2/ECS/Lambda) orchestrated by CodePipeline. Deployment strategies: in-place, blue/green, canary, linear. CloudFormation for infrastructure deployment with change sets and rollback.

CloudWatch (metrics, logs, alarms, dashboards), X-Ray (distributed tracing), CloudWatch Synthetics (canary monitoring), ServiceLens (unified view), Contributor Insights (top-N contributors), and Application Insights (automated monitoring setup for common patterns).

Discovering on-premises servers for migration planning — Agentless (VMware vCenter, basic info) and Agent-based (detailed performance data, network dependencies). Data feeds into Migration Hub for planning and AWS Migration Evaluator for business case.

Building a business case for migration — analyzing on-premises infrastructure data to project AWS costs, identify right-sizing opportunities, and model different migration scenarios with projected savings and TCO comparisons.

Phase approach — Assess (discovery, business case), Mobilize (landing zone, pilot), Migrate & Modernize (wave-based migration). Use MGN for rehost, DMS for databases, DataSync for storage, and Snow Family for massive data. Parallel workstreams for speed.

Running VMware workloads natively on AWS infrastructure — using familiar VMware tools (vCenter, vSAN, NSX) with direct access to AWS services. Use for rapid datacenter evacuation, disaster recovery, or extending VMware environments to AWS without re-architecting.

AWS infrastructure and services on-premises — fully managed racks or servers running AWS compute, storage, and databases locally. Use for low-latency requirements, local data processing, data residency, and consistent hybrid experience with the same APIs and tools.

AWS compute and storage at 5G network edges — ultra-low latency for mobile applications, AR/VR, real-time gaming, and IoT. Wavelength Zones extend VPCs to carrier locations, accessed via carrier gateway.

AWS infrastructure closer to end users in metro areas — extending a Region for latency-sensitive workloads like real-time gaming, media rendering, and live streaming. Subnets in Local Zones are part of the parent Region's VPC.

Outposts (AWS on-premises), Local Zones (metro edge), Wavelength (5G edge), Snow Family (disconnected edge), ECS/EKS Anywhere (containers on-premises), SSM for hybrid management, and Direct Connect + VPN for connectivity.

Containerizing applications — App2Container (Java/.NET to containers), Copilot CLI (ECS deployment), EKS Anywhere (on-premises Kubernetes), ECR (container registry), and choosing ECS vs EKS based on Kubernetes requirements and operational complexity tolerance.

A CLI tool that containerizes existing Java and .NET applications — analyzing running applications, generating Dockerfiles and container images, creating ECS/EKS deployment artifacts, and optionally setting up CI/CD pipelines.

Moving to serverless — Lambda for compute (from EC2/containers), DynamoDB for databases (from self-managed NoSQL), API Gateway for APIs (from self-managed web servers), Step Functions for workflows, and EventBridge for event routing.

Homogeneous (same engine, DMS only), Heterogeneous (different engines, SCT + DMS), consolidation (multiple sources to one target), and continuous replication for near-zero downtime. Consider: Aurora for MySQL/PostgreSQL, DynamoDB for NoSQL, and Redshift for data warehouse.

Migrating mainframe workloads — Replatform (AWS Blu Age for automated refactoring to Java) or Rehost (Micro Focus for running COBOL on AWS). Includes managed runtime environments, data migration tools, and application testing capabilities.

Running SAP workloads — certified EC2 instances (x2idn for HANA), EBS io2 for high IOPS, dedicated hosts for licensing, AWS Launch Wizard for guided deployment, Backint for SAP HANA backups to S3, and AWS Systems Manager for SAP for operations.

Migrating Windows — MGN for lift-and-shift, FSx for Windows File Server (SMB/NTFS/AD), RDS for SQL Server, AD Connector or AWS Managed Microsoft AD for directory services, and License Manager for tracking BYOL compliance.

Affordable DR with continuous replication — block-level replication from on-premises/cloud to AWS staging area, point-in-time recovery, non-disruptive drills, and rapid failover/failback. RPO seconds, RTO minutes. Replaces CloudEndure Disaster Recovery.
